title: Multiply 2-digits by 1-digit with distributive property
description: Multiply a 1-digit number by a multi-digit number by decomposing the multi-digit number.
topic_path: Multi-digit multiplication: place value and area models

assessment_items.question_content:
**Fill in the blanks to solve  $7 \times 28$.**

||
:-|
|$\purpleE{7} \times \maroonE{28}$|
$=$ | $ \purpleE{7}\times(\maroonE{20} + {}$ [[☃ numeric-input 13]] $)$|
$=$ | $( \purpleE{7}\times\maroonE{20}) + (\purpleE7\times \maroonE{8}$  $)$|
$=$ | $140 +{56}$ |
$=$| [[☃ numeric-input 14]]|
hints: We can break up $28$ to make it easier to multiply.
$\purpleD{7} \times \maroonE{28} = \purpleD{7}\times(\maroonE{20} + \maroonE{8})$

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{7} \times \maroonE{28} &= \purpleD{7}\times(\maroonE{20} + \maroonE{8})\\\\
&=( \purpleD{7}\times\maroonE{20}) + (\purpleD7\times \maroonE{8})
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{7} \times \maroonE{28} &= \purpleD{7}\times(\maroonE{20} + \maroonE{8})\\\\
&=( \purpleD{7}\times\maroonE{20}) + (\purpleD7\times \maroonE{8})\\\\
&=140 + 56
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{7} \times \maroonE{28} &= \purpleD{7}\times(\maroonE{20} + \maroonE{8})\\\\
&=( \purpleD{7}\times\maroonE{20}) + (\purpleD7\times \maroonE{8})\\\\
&=140 + 56\\\\
&=196
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Fill in the blanks to solve  $6 \times 52$.**

||
:-|
|$\purpleD{6} \times \maroonE{52}$|
$=$|$ \purpleD{6}\times(\maroonE{50}+ \maroonE{2})$|
$=$|$( \purpleD{6}\times$ [[☃ numeric-input 14]] $)+ (\purpleD6\times \maroonE2)$|
$=$| ${300}+ 12$|
$=$| [[☃ numeric-input 15]]|
hints: We can break up $52$ to make it easier to multiply.
$\purpleD{6} \times \maroonE{52} = \purpleD{6}\times(\maroonE{50} + \maroonE{2})$

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{6} \times \maroonE{52} &= \purpleD{6}\times(\maroonE{50} + \maroonE{2})\\\\
&=( \purpleD{6}\times\maroonE{50}) + (\purpleD6\times \maroonE{2})
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{6} \times \maroonE{52} &= \purpleD{6}\times(\maroonE{50} + \maroonE{2})\\\\
&=( \purpleD{6}\times\maroonE{50}) + (\purpleD6\times \maroonE{2})\\\\
&=300 + 12
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{6} \times \maroonE{52} &= \purpleD{6}\times(\maroonE{50} + \maroonE{2})\\\\
&=( \purpleD{6}\times\maroonE{50}) + (\purpleD6\times \maroonE{2})\\\\
&=300 + 12\\\\
&=312
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Fill in the blanks to solve  $39 \times 4$.**

||
:-|
|$\purpleD{39} \times \maroonE{4}$|
$=$|$(\purpleD{30}+ \purpleD9){}\times \maroonE{4}$|
$=$|$( \purpleD{30}\times\maroonE4) + (\purpleD9 \times \maroonE{4})$|
$=$| [[☃ numeric-input 1]]${}+ 36$|
$=$| [[☃ numeric-input 2]]|
hints: We can break up $39$ to make it easier to multiply.
$\purpleD{39} \times \maroonE{4} = (\purpleD{30} +\purpleD9) \times\maroonE{4}$

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{39} \times \maroonE{4} &= (\purpleD{30} +\purpleD9) \times\maroonE{4}\\\\
&=( \purpleD{30}\times\maroonE{4}) + (\purpleD9\times \maroonE{4})
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{39} \times \maroonE{4} &= (\purpleD{30} +\purpleD9) \times\maroonE{4}\\\\
&=( \purpleD{30}\times\maroonE{4}) + (\purpleD9\times \maroonE{4})\\\\
&=120 + 36
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{39} \times \maroonE{4} &= (\purpleD{30} +\purpleD9) \times\maroonE{4}\\\\
&=( \purpleD{30}\times\maroonE{4}) + (\purpleD9\times \maroonE{4})\\\\
&=120 + 36\\\\
&=156
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Fill in the blanks to solve  $19 \times 6$.**

||
-:|:-
|$\purpleE{19} \times \redE{6}$|
$=$|$\purpleE{10}{\text \redE{ sixes}} +{}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $\text \redE{ sixes}$|
$=$|$ {60} + 54$   |
$=$| [[☃ input-number 3]]|
hints: $\purpleE{19}\times\redE6$ is the same as $\purpleE{19}\redE{\text{ sixes}}$.
We can break up $\purpleE{19}$ to make it easier to multiply.
$\purpleE{19} \redE{\text{ sixes}} =  {\text{\purpleE{10} \redE{sixes}}} + {\text{\purpleE{9} \redE{sixes}}}$

$\begin{align}
\purpleE{19} \redE{\text{ sixes}} &=  {\text{\purpleE{10} \redE{sixes}}} + {\text{\purpleE{9} \redE{sixes}}}\\\\
&={} {60} + 54
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\purpleE{19} \redE{\text{ sixes}} &=  {\text{\purpleE{10} \redE{sixes}}} + {\text{\purpleE{9} \redE{sixes}}}\\\\
&={} {60} + 54\\\\
&=114
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Fill in the blanks to solve  $48 \times 8$.**

||
:-|
|$\purpleD{48} \times \maroonE{8}$|
$=$|$ {\text{\purpleD{40} \maroonE{eights}}} +\purpleD{8}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]]|
$=$|   ${320}+ {64}$|
$=$| [[☃ numeric-input 20]]|
hints: $\purpleD{48}\times\maroonE8$ is the same as $\purpleD{48}\maroonE{\text{ eights}}$.
We can break up $\purpleD{48}$ to make it easier to multiply.
$\purpleD{48} \maroonE{\text{ eights}} =  {\text{\purpleD{40} \maroonE{eights}}} + {\text{\purpleD{8} \maroonE{eights}}}$

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{48} \maroonE{\text{ eights}} &=  {\text{\purpleD{40} \maroonE{eights}}} + {\text{\purpleD{8} \maroonE{eights}}}\\\\
&={320} + 64
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{48} \maroonE{\text{ eights}} &=  {\text{\purpleD{40} \maroonE{eights}}} + {\text{\purpleD{8} \maroonE{eights}}}\\\\
&={320} + 64\\\\
&=384
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Fill in the blanks to solve  $32\times 9$.**

||
:-|
|$\purpleD{32} \times \maroonE{9}$|
$=$|  $\purpleD{30}\maroonE{\text{ nines}} +\purpleD{2}\maroonE{\text{ nines}}$|
$=$ |[[☃ numeric-input 18]]  ${}+{18}$|
$=$|[[☃ numeric-input 19]] |
hints: $\purpleD{32}\times\maroonE9$ is the same as $\purpleD{32}\maroonE{\text{ nines}}$.
We can break up $\purpleD{32}$ to make it easier to multiply.
$\purpleD{32} \maroonE{\text{ nines}} =  {\text{\purpleD{30} \maroonE{nines}}} + {\text{\purpleD{2} \maroonE{nines}}}$

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{32} \maroonE{\text{ nines}} &=  {\text{\purpleD{30} \maroonE{nines}}} + {\text{\purpleD{2} \maroonE{nines}}}\\\\
&={} {270} + 18
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{32} \maroonE{\text{ nines}}& =  {\text{\purpleD{30} \maroonE{nines}}} + {\text{\purpleD{2} \maroonE{nines}}}\\\\
&={} {270} + 18\\\\
&=288
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
DeShawn tried to evaluate $4\times82$ step by step. However, DeShawn made a mistake.


 | |$\purpleD{4}\times\maroonE{82}$
- | -
${\text{Step } 1}$ | $=$|$\purpleD4 \times (\maroonE{80}+\maroonE2)$ 
${\text{Step } 2}$ | $=$|$(\purpleD4 +\maroonE{80}) + (\purpleD4 +\maroonE{2}) $
${\text{Step } 3}$ |$=$|$84+6$
${\text{Step } 4}$ | $=$|$90$

**Find DeShawn's first mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
DeShawn showed a lot of strong math thinking. He tried to break $82$ up to make it easier to multiply.

Let's find where DeShawn changed the value of the expression so it was **not** equal to $\purpleD4\times\maroonE{82}$.

###Step 1

$\purpleD4\times\maroonE{82} \ {\large\overset{\checkmark}{=}} \ \purpleD4\times(\maroonE{80}+\maroonE2)$

###Step 2

$\purpleD4\times\maroonE{82}  \neq (\purpleD4 \redD+\maroonE{80}) + (\purpleD4 \redD+\maroonE{2}) $

DeShawn **incorrectly** put a $\redD{+}$ instead of $\times$ inside each set of parentheses.
DeShawn made a mistake in step $2$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Cass tried to evaluate $9\times76$ step by step. However, Cass made a mistake.

 | |$\purpleD9\times\maroonE{76}$
- | -
${\text{Step } 1}$ | $=$|$\purpleD9 \times (\maroonE{70} + \maroonE{6})$ 
${\text{Step } 2}$ | $=$|$(\purpleD9 \times \maroonE{70}) + (\purpleD9 \times \maroonE{6}) $
${\text{Step } 3}$ |$=$|$63 + 54$
${\text{Step } 4}$ | $=$|$117$

**Find Cass's first mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Cass showed a lot of strong math thinking. They tried to break $76$ up to make it easier to multiply.

Let's find where Cass changed the value of the expression so it was **not** equal to $\purpleD{9}\times\maroonE{76}$.

###Step 1

$\purpleD9\times\maroonE{76}~ {\large\overset{\checkmark}{=}}  ~\purpleD9 \times (\maroonE{70} + \maroonE{6})$

###Step 2

$\purpleD9 \times (\maroonE{70} + \maroonE{6})~ {\large\overset{\checkmark}{=}}  ~(\purpleD9 \times \maroonE{70}) + (\purpleD9 \times \maroonE{6})$

###Step 3

$(\purpleD9 \times \maroonE{70}) + (\purpleD9 \times \maroonE{6}) \neq 63 + 54$ 

Cass **incorrectly** multiplied $\purpleD9\times\maroonE{70}$ and got $63$ instead of $630$.
Cass made a mistake in step $3$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Alondra tried to evaluate $45\times3$ step by step. However, Alondra made a mistake.

 || $\purpleD{45}\times\maroonE{3}$
- | -
${\text{Step } 1}$ | $=$|$\purpleD{4} \maroonE{\text{ threes}}+ \purpleD{5} \maroonE{\text{ threes}}$
${\text{Step } 2}$ |$=$|$12 + 15$
${\text{Step } 3}$ | $=$|$27$

**Find Alondra's first mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Alondra showed a lot of strong math thinking. She tried to break $45$ up to make it easier to multiply.

Let's find where Alondra changed the value of the expression so it was **not** equal to $\purpleD{45}\times\maroonE{3}$.

###Step 1

$\purpleD{45}\times\maroonE{3} \neq \purpleD{4} \maroonE{\text{ threes}}+ \purpleD{5} \maroonE{\text{ threes}}$

Alondra **incorrectly** broke up $\purpleD{45}$. She got $\purpleD{4}$ and $\purpleD{5}$ instead of $\purpleD{40}$ and $\purpleD{5}$.
Alondra made a mistake in step $1$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which two expressions are equivalent to $42\text{ eights}$? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\purpleD{42}\redE{\text{ eights}}$ is the same as $\purpleD{42}\times\redE{8}$ or $\redE{8} \times \purpleD{42}$.

Let's use the distributive property to find other ways to represent $\redE{8}\times \purpleD{42}$.

We can break apart $\purpleD{42}$ into two numbers that add to $\purpleD{42}$.

$\maroonE8\times\purpleD{42} = \maroonE8\times (\purpleD{40} + \purpleD2)$

Next, we can distribute the $\maroonE8$ to each addend of $\purpleD{42}$.

$\maroonE8\times\purpleD{42}  = (\maroonE8\times\purpleD{40})+  \  (\maroonE8\times\purpleD{2})$

$42\text{ eights}$ is equivalent to the following expressions:

- $\maroonE8\times (\purpleD{40} + \purpleD2)$

- $(\maroonE8\times\purpleD{40})+ (\maroonE8\times\purpleD{2})$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which two expressions are equivalent to $87\text{ fives}$? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\purpleD{87}\redE{\text{ fives}}$ is the same as $\purpleD{87}\times\redE{5}$ or $\redE{5} \times \purpleD{87}$.

Let's use the distributive property to find other ways to represent $\maroonE5\times\purpleD{87}$.

We can break apart $\purpleD{87}$ into two numbers that add to $\purpleD{87}$.

$\maroonE5\times\purpleD{87} = \maroonE5\times (\purpleD{80} + \purpleD7)$

We can distribute the $\maroonE5$ to each addend of $\purpleD{87}$.

$\maroonE5\times\purpleD{87}  = (\maroonE5\times\purpleD{80})+ (\maroonE5\times\purpleD{7})$

$87\text{ fives}$ is equivalent to the following expressions:

- $\maroonE5\times (\purpleD{80} + \purpleD7)$

- $(\maroonE5\times\purpleD{80})+ (\maroonE5\times\purpleD{7})$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which two expressions are equivalent to $64\text{ nines}$? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\purpleD{64}\maroonE{\text{ nines}}$ is the same as $\purpleD{64}\times\maroonE{9}$ or $\maroonE{9} \times \purpleD{64}$.

Let's use the distributive property to find other ways to represent $\purpleD{64}\times\maroonE{9}$.

We can break apart $\purpleD{64}$ into two numbers that add to $\purpleD{64}$.

$\purpleD{64}\times\maroonE9 = (\purpleD{60} + \purpleD4) \times\maroonE9$

We can distribute the $\maroonE9$ to each addend of $\purpleD{64}$.

$\purpleD{64}\times\maroonE9  = (\purpleD{60}\times\maroonE9)+  (\purpleD{4}\times\maroonE9)$

$64\text{ nines}$ is equivalent to the following expressions:

- $(\purpleD{60}\times\maroonE9)+ 
(\purpleD{4}\times\maroonE9)$

- $(\purpleD{60} + \purpleD4) \times\maroonE9$
The expression $\purpleD{42} \times \blueE{2}$ is the same as $\purpleD{42}\blueE{\text{ twos}}$.

We can break apart $\purpleE{42}$ into two numbers that add to $\purpleE{42}$.

$\purpleD{42}\blueE{\text{ twos}} = \purpleD{40}\blueE{\text{ twos}} + \purpleD{2}\blueE{\text{ twos}}$

$42\times 2$ is equivalent to the following expression:

- $\purpleD{40}\blueE{\text{ twos}} + \purpleD{2}\blueE{\text{ twos}}$

*Note:* There are many other equivalent expressions, but this was the only one in the list.
The expression $\blueE{8} \times \purpleD{93}$ is the same as $\purpleD{93}\blueE{\text{ eights}}$.

We can break apart $\purpleE{93}$ into two numbers that add to $\purpleE{93}$.

$\purpleD{93}\blueE{\text{ eights}} = \purpleD{90}\blueE{\text{ eights}} + \purpleD{3}\blueE{\text{ eights}}$

$8\times 93$ is equivalent to the following expression:

- $\purpleD{90}\blueE{\text{ eights}} + \purpleD{3}\blueE{\text{ eights}}$

*Note:* There are many other equivalent expressions, but this was the only one in the list.
The expression $\purpleD{77} \times \blueE{4}$ is the same as $\purpleD{77}\blueE{\text{ fours}}$.

We can break apart $\purpleE{77}$ into two numbers that add to $\purpleE{77}$.

$\purpleD{77}\blueE{\text{ fours}} = \purpleD{70}\blueE{\text{ fours}} + \purpleD{7}\blueE{\text{ fours}}$

$77\times 4$ is equivalent to the following expression:

- $\purpleD{70}\blueE{\text{ fours}} + \purpleD{7}\blueE{\text{ fours}}$

*Note:* There are many other equivalent expressions, but this was the only one in the list.
